Coding review:    The coding for C6 seems contradictory: it mentions that people are prohibited from leaving home (implying at all times) and that there is a curfew from 8pm to 5am. If people are not allowed to leave their home at all, then an additional curfew doesn’t make sense.    Unfortunately, the archived source offers no additional clarification, as it mentions neither they prohibition to leave home nor the curfew. I also can’t find any archived information on the government’s website (https://gouvernement.cg/), that would shed more light on exactly what the policy was at the time.    If the curfew is only at night, then this by itself would not change the H8 coding, as per the coding guidance “will not affect the operation of Long Term Care Facilities or elderly residents, so really only significant curfews during the majority of daytime hours would warrant coding C6 as a 2, and an H8 as a non-zero value accordingly.”    However, a later coding in July has an archived UK government foreign travel advice page, which mentions exactly what had been coded for the period from 28 March onwards:  •	Lockdown measures, including prohibiting people from leaving home, suspension of non-essential travel and a curfew. There is an exception for workers providing essential goods and services.  •	A nationwide curfew from 8 pm until 5 am.    This only seems to make sense if people are told to stay at home in general, where there are more exceptions during the day than during the curfew times.  http://archive.vn/07PUl    Interpreted in this way, this does indeed impact the elderly as well.    Therefore, changing H8 to align with C6.    Note that C6 seems to have been coded inconsistently. The same policies – quoted above for March and July – are coded as 3G in the March to May period, and as 2G in the June to September period.  It might make sense to revisit the C6 coding, and if changes are made, then change H8 accordingly.

Coding review:    C6 coded as 2G. However, the curfew in the notes and archived sources only applies in certain parts of the country.   So should be probably coded as 2T, although a later coding source from the UK foreign office still mentions a nationwide curfew. It looks like the 3rd party information available at the time – since no official government source has been archived – is conflicting. Hence I am not making any coding revision for C6 at this time, and this may need to be revisited if additional information can be found for C6.    More importantly for H8, there is also no mention of a stay at home order anymore, so leaving H8 at 0 per the coding guidance that a nighttime curfew “will not affect the operation of Long Term Care Facilities or elderly residents, so really only significant curfews during the majority of daytime hours would warrant coding C6 as a 2, and an H8 as a non-zero value accordingly.”

According to the IMF, The government has adopted some measures to ease tax and duty payments for private enterprises. In particular, more time has been given to companies to pay their taxes and tax assessments on site have been abandoned. The import duty directorate is also strongly encouraging electronic payment of dues and allowing more electronic documents to be accepted at the port. Corporate income tax has been reduced to 28 percent from 30 percent and the turnover tax has been reduced to 5 percent from 7 percent for small businesses with turnover below 100 million XAF.    It cannot be determined that these policies are still in place and to what extent at this time.    Please see: https://web.archive.org/web/20201123175722/https://www.imf.org/en/Topics/imf-and-covid19/Policy-Responses-to-COVID-19

Country-Specific Information:    There is a nationwide curfew in the DRC each evening from 9 pm to 5 am. Masks are required in public spaces.  Businesses, schools, airports, and ports are open.  While DRC permits borders open, many neighboring countries continue to restrict border crossings.    Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210405200921/https://cd.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

Domestic Restrictions:    Gatherings of more than three people in public and private spaces are prohibited during the state of health emergency.    Marketplaces may open five days a week in Brazzaville and Pointe-Noire.    In Brazzaville and Pointe-Noire, a curfew is in effect 23:00-05:00 Monday to Friday and 20:00-05:00 Saturday to Sunday. There is no curfew in the rest of the country.    Restaurants and bars can reopen but measures must be taken to accommodate customers in outside areas if possible.    Security personnel have increased screening measures, which will be conducted throughout the country's entry and exit points.    The wearing of facemasks is mandatory in public spaces.    Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210419183227/https://www.garda.com/crisis24/news-alerts/467301/congo-covid-19-related-state-of-health-emergency-extended-for-20-days-from-april-17-update-22

Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210419185611/https://cg.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

Country-Specific Information:    The Republic of Congo has confirmed cases of COVID-19 since March 14, 2020. As of March 31, there were 10,084 confirmed cases, 9,286 recoveries and 137 confirmed deaths.  The government of The Republic of Congo extended a State of Health Emergency until May 4 to limit the spread of COVID-19. The curfew in Brazzaville and Pointe Noire is from 23:00 until 05:00, Mondays through Fridays and from 20:00 until 05:00 on Saturdays, Sundays and holidays.  The Congolese air borders opened on August 24 for international flights. International air carriers are resuming some services into and from Brazzaville and Pointe-Noire.  Land and river borders remain closed.  Places of worship, schools, restaurants and bars are open.  All international passengers are required to self-quarantine for two weeks in their residence or hotel for 14 day upon arrival. Passengers in transit will be quarantined at their expense at designated facilities until they resume their travels.    Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210419185611/https://cg.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

Vaccination started on 19 April, initially with 300,000 doses of Sinopharm's BBIBP-CorV vaccine donated by China and 12,000 doses of Sputnik V. By the end of the month (30th April 2021) 41379 persons had received their first inoculation and 11700 had been fully vaccinated. Vaccination started in Pointe-Noire on Monday, April 19, 2021. To date, five (5) vaccination centers are in operation there. In Brazzaville, four (4) new vaccination centers (Madibou, Mfilou, Moukondo and WHO Africa) have been added to the eleven (11) existing centers. Vaccination operations have extended to Owando and Makoua, whose centers opened on April 26 and April 30, 2021, respectively. It is not clear which groups are being targeted for vaccines, if any.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517190932/https://www.adiac-congo.com/content/communique-de-la-coordination-nationale-de-gestion-de-la-pandemie-de-coronavirus-covid-19-15

As part of the decisons taken at the end of 34th Meeting of THE NATIONAL COORDINATION FOR THE MANAGEMENT OF THE CORONAVIRUS COVID-19 PANDEMIC on February 28, 2022, recommendations were made for "the abolition of the mandatory tests hitherto carried out when entering Congolese territory (at airports, etc.)" and "require the presentation, at the border, of a negative RT-PCR test of less than 72 hours at most for all passengers entering Congo, with the exception of children under ten (10) years old". Some of the renewed measures introduced on February 28 2022 include: (1) Organize regular screening of the people most at risk of contamination; and  (2) Regularly perform rapid nasopharyngeal antigen tests in all departments of the country for routine screening of covid-19. The Republic of the Congo national vaccination programme started in March 2021 and is using the Sputnik V, Sinopharm, and Janssen (Johnson and Johnson) vaccines. British nationals resident in the Republic of the Congo are eligible for vaccination, depending on available stocks, if they choose to join the programme. Further information on the vaccination programme can be found at the emergency Public Health Operations Centre of the Ministry of Health and Population, or at the vaccination centre at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Iceland announced on 22 Dec 2021 that:  'Vaccination against COVID-19 is planned to begin in Iceland on 29 December. It is uncertain whether it can start on that day in the whole country, but it depends on the transport of the vaccine out to land, the weather conditions and the health care facilities. Frontline staff in the health service, which numbers more than 1,000 people, will be in the first group, and at the same time vaccination will be started among the residents of the nursing and geriatric wards, which number 3,000-4,000 people. When another shipment arrives in the country, vaccination will continue with the oldest age groups.'    https://archive.vn/6dZeR

A, C:    Iceland received the first doses of COVID-19 vaccine on December 28 and vaccination began on December 29. At the moment, Iceland is expecting to receive vaccine for at least 30,000 people before the end of March.  COVID-19 vaccination is optional and free of charge in Iceland. Vaccines are being administered according to priority groups defined by health authorities, though the Chief Epidemiologist may grant exceptions to that order. The first to be vaccinated were frontline healthcare workers and nursing home residents. Iceland said on 4 May 2021 that:   'In Iceland, pregnant women have not been identified in the risk group for COVID-19 infection due to pregnancy. Pre-vaccinated women who have already been vaccinated have been able to be vaccinated despite pregnancy, but it is recommended that they be vaccinated during the second or third trimester of pregnancy but not when organ formation is at its peak in the first trimester. Abroad, in some places the same policy is followed and in some places pregnant women are seen as a priority group in the COVID-19 vaccination. In some countries, the policy is not to vaccinate pregnant women.  The greatest experience and good is from the use of the mRNA vaccines for pregnant women since they were first on the market. Quote: "Authorities in Iceland have extended the nation's COVID-19-related international entry restrictions until at least July 1. Under most circumstances, travelers from EU and Schengen Area states, as well as those from Australia, Israel, Japan, New Zealand, Rwanda, Singapore, South Korea, and Thailand, can enter Iceland. Most travelers from other locations will remain prohibited from entering with some exceptions, including freight and transport workers, immediate family members of Icelandic residents, diplomats, students, individuals conducting necessary work, and persons traveling for urgent family reasons. Permitted arrivals must complete a preregistration form before departing for Iceland and present a negative result from a COVID-19 polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test taken within 72 hours prior to departure for Iceland. In addition, arrivals must undergo a COVID-19 test at their point of entry and quarantine for five days before taking a second test; travelers without adequate accommodation for self-isolation will be placed in government quarantine facilities."  Archive Link  http://web.archive.org/web/20210621175425/https://www.garda.com/crisis24/news-alerts/490751/iceland-authorities-extend-covid-19-related-travel-restrictions-until-july-1-update-17  _______________________________________________________________________  Quote: "Passengers vaccinated against COVID-19 or recovered from it  Yes, until June 30, if you hold an appropriate certificate showing full vaccination or previous infection, you are required to undergo one COVID-19 test upon arrival. The test is free of charge. You must wait for the result of this test (usually received within 5-6 hours but may take up to 24 hours) at your accommodation. You do not need to quarantine for 5-6 days and take a second test."  "From July 1, 2021, there is no longer a requirement for arrival testing for passengers fully vaccinated against COVID-19 or recovered from it.    Passengers not vaccinated against COVID-19 and not previously infected  It is mandatory for these passengers to undergo a double COVID-testing procedure along with quarantine for 5-6 days."  Archive Link   http://web.archive.org/web/20210621184330/https://www.icelandair.com/en-ca/blog/iceland-is-open-faq/

Quote: "The Government has decided to pay a special supplement over and above basic unemployment benefits next year, so as to support the large group of people whose income-linked unemployment benefits will expire in the next few months. The 2.5% supplement will be paid in addition to the 3.6% increase provided for in the 2021 fiscal budget proposal. With these increases, which total 6.2%, basic unemployment benefits will equal ISK 307,403 per month.    Increased payments for support of job-seekers’ children will be extended through the end of 2021, with a 6% supplement added to basic unemployment benefits for each child instead of the previous 4%. Moreover, a December supplement of just over ISK 86,000 will be paid to confirmed job-seekers."  "The Government of Iceland has announced various measures to mitigate the economic and societal effects of the coronavirus outbreak.  Resilience subsidies, For entities suffering revenue losses between 60% and 80%, Support for families with children, Support for disability pensioners and vulnerable groups." https://web.archive.org/web/20211114095450/https://www.covid.is/sub-categories/effective-restrictions  At all workplaces and when performing other professional activities, individuals who are unrelated must keep a distance of 1 metre from each other.    At gatherings where all guest are seated the 1 metre rule is not effective, but a face mask must be worn.  Limitations to operations  Pharmacies, grocery stores and other stores may accept up to 50 people. In addition 5 persons are allowed for each 10 m2 of space to the maximum of 500 customers in their premises, as long as it is possible to ensure at least 1 metre between persons who are unrelated.    Performing arts, cinemas and other cultural events may have up to 50 persons on stage, i.e. during rehearsals and shows. Up to 50 seated attendees may be accommodated in a given area, and they must wear face masks. Seats must be numbered and registered with the attendee’s name, ID No. and telephone number. The 1 metre rule can be deviated from while seated. Children born in 2016 and later are not included in this number. Refreshments may not be sold during intervals. Up to 500 people may be allowed in each area provided they present a negative rapid antigen test taken no longer than 48 hours in advance at a certified test center.    Please refer to the special rules for admitting up to 500 people in a given area, subject to rapid antigen test.    Ceremonies held by religious and life stance associations may be attended by up to 50 persons. The same applies to wakes, confirmation parties and similar events. Up to 500 persons may attend ceremonies held by religious and life stance associations; stage, cultural and sports events; conferences, lectures and comparable events, provided they present a negative rapid antigen test taken no less than 48 hours earlier, from a certified test center.       Restaurants where alcoholic beverages are allowed may stay open until 22:00 all days of the week with a maximum of 50 guests in each area and must maintain the 1 meter distance rule. All guests must be registered by name, ID No. and telephone number. Alcoholic beverages are to be served to seated guests. All guests must have left the location at 23:00.    Nightclubs and bars may be open until 22:00 all days of the week with a maximum of 50 guests in each area. Alcoholic beverages are to be served to seated guests. All guests must be registered by name, ID No. and telephone number. All guests must have left the location at 23:00.    Gambling machines and arcades may be open to 22:00 all days of the week with a maximum of 50 guests in each area. Alcoholic beverages are to be served to seated guests. All guests must have left the location at 23:00.    Private gatherings at premises licensed to serve alcohol may not continue after 23.00.    Temporary permits may not be granted for social gatherings, such as concerts, dances, bonfires or other events which may be expected to draw crowds after 23:00.    Swimming and bathing pools, spas and gyms are open for 75% of permitted number of people. Equipment must be disinfected between users and all users must be registered. Children born 2016 and later are not included in this number.    Ski slopes are permitted to accept 75% of their maximum acceptance limit for each area. Children born in 2016 and later are not included in this number.

Quarantine is used when an individual has possibly been infected with COVID-19 but is not yet symptomatic. Isolation is for those who have a confirmed COVID-19 infection but do not need a hospital stay. Special precaution is used when an individual has possibly been exposed to COVID-19 but quarantine is not considered necessary. The Tracing Team assesses the risk each time, often in collaboration with school administrators or employers.    It’s important that people take action themselves if they know that they have been exposed to an infection (i.e. have met someone infected), because then there is a chance that people will get sick and infect others. They should then go into quarantine immediately and wait for a message from the Tracing Team, which formally registers people in quarantine.  https://web.archive.org/web/20211114063221/https://www.covid.is/sub-categories/quarantine    Note: Iceland is using the Rakning C-19 app.  Infection tracing is a social issue. The C-19 app is an important tool in the comprehensive work carried out here in Iceland with regard to COVID-19 and can be of great assistance to hinder the spread of the virus.  https://web.archive.org/web/20211114063637/https://www.covid.is/app/en

The following rules are in force from 5. November 2021 and will apply up to and including 9. November 2021.    Restrictions on gatherings:  The maximum number of people allowed in the same location is 2000 persons, with certain restrictions, both in public and private locations, outdoors and indoors. Unlimited number of people may attend an event if all guests bring a negative result from a rapid antigen test. Restrictions on the number of people do not apply to children born 2006 or later.   https://web.archive.org/web/20211111082710/https://www.covid.is/sub-categories/effective-restrictions

The Regulation on restrictions on gatherings due the pandemic will apply to 8 December 2021.  Restrictions to the number of people at gatherings:  The maximum number of people allowed in the same location is 50 persons, with certain restrictions, both in public and private locations, outdoors and indoors. Restrictions on the number of people and the social distancing rule do not apply to children born 2016 or later.    Up to 500 people may attend an event if additional conditions are met:  -All attendees must present a negative rapid antigen test taken no longer than 48 hours in advance, from a certified test center. Note that self-tests are not accepted.  -1 meter distancing rule except when seated.  -All guests must be registered stating their name, ID No. and telephone number. This information must be kept on file for two weeks. If guests are in numbered seats they must be registered.  -Face masks must be worn indoors wherever the 1 metre social distancing rule cannot be observed. This rule does not apply at school dances. Children born 2006 and later are exempt from wearing a mask.  -Refreshments may not be sold during intervals.  https://web.archive.org/web/20211113223052/https://www.covid.is/sub-categories/effective-restrictions

For residents of Iceland:  Show a certificate of full vaccination , or a certificate of previous infection, at the border. If a certificate is not considered valid, two samples must be taken and a 5-day quarantine in between.  For tourists not from Iceland:  Vaccinated tourists, and those with a confirmed previous infection, must present a negative COVID test before boarding a plane or ship on their way to Iceland. Both the PCR test and the COVID-19 rapid antigen test are accepted. No self-tests are accepted. The test must be taken within 72 hours before departure on the first leg of the trip. If a certificate is not presented at the border, ISK 100,000 is attached. guilt and duty to go to border sampling.  Additionally, if from a high risk area:  Everyone needs to be sampled upon arrival in the country, including children born in 2015 and earlier. Sampling and quarantine: Everyone born in 2015 and earlier must hold a five-day quarantine and go for another sampling at the end of it. Sampling is free. Go directly to the quarantine station from the border station by airport bus, taxi, rental car or private car. You may not be picked up at the airport.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211206181328/https://www.covid.is/undirflokkar/boluefni-eda-motefni  https://web.archive.org/web/20211206181754/https://www.covid.is/undirflokkar/fra-ha-ahaettusvaedi

21/2/22    People that have been infected with COVID-19 or have been vaccinated  Travel restrictions do not apply to foreign citizens who can provide appropriate proof of vaccination against COVID-19 or a prior COVID-19 infection that fulfills requirements set forth by the Chief Epidemiologist    Rules at the border are in force until February 28th 2022.    Rules at the border for people with vaccination or former COVID-19 infection  All travellers must pre-register before arriving in Iceland and confirm their departure day, if available.  Present a certficate of full vaccination, or a certificate of previous infection, at the border.  If a certificate is not valid the individual needs to undergo 2 tests and quarantine for 5 days in between.  NOTE. From 1 February 2022, a vaccination certificate is valid if no more than 270 days (9 months) have elapsed from the primary vaccination when an individual has not undergone a booster vaccination. Primary vaccination is considered complete after two doses of vaccine or after one Janssen injection. A booster dose cancels this expiration date.  Those who live in Iceland, or who have a network here, must go for COVID-19 testing within 48 hours of arriving in Iceland. They do not have to present a negative COVID-19 test at the border. Testing can be obtained at the border in Keflavík or at the health care centres all over Iceland. There is no need to quarantine while waiting for the results of this test, but travellers are asked to attend to personal infection control, limit contact with vulnerable individuals and be alert to the symptoms of COVID-19.  Rules for travellers without a network in Iceland: Vaccinated passengers, and those with prior infection of COVID-19, need to present a negative covid-19 test either a PCR or Rapid antigen test. NB. self-tests are not accepted. The test must have been taken within 72 hours before departure on the first leg of the journey. A fine for not presenting a negative COVID-19 test at the border is 100.000 ISK.  If a traveller has recently been diagnosed with COVID-19, they do not need to present a negative PCR or rapid antigen test at the border. Instead, a positive PCR test must be presented that is older than 7 days (isolation completed) but younger than 180 days. These individuals do not have to take a rapid antigen test or PCR test within 48 hours due to their connection with the country.  Testing and quarantine of children: Children under the age of 18 are permitted to travel to Iceland with their parents, given the parents are exempt from travel restrictions to Iceland. However, children born in or before 2004 need to undergo testing at the border, quarantine for 5 days and then be tested a second time, unless they have a certificate of full vaccination. Children born in 2005 or later are exempt from the rules at the border.  It is recommended that travellers download the Rakning C-19 app. It is used, amongst other things, to communicate negative test results and helps to trace infections if necessary.  If the slightest suspicion arises that an individual has presented a forged certificate, he/she/they will be obliged to be tested twice with quarantine in between.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220221215242/https://www.covid.is/sub-categories/vaccination-and-antibodies

All travelers including Tanzanians and returning residents will be required to present a  negative COVID-19 test certificate upon arrival. The only accepted test is Real Time  Reverse Transcription Polymerase Chain Reaction (RT-PCR) testing or Nucleic Acid  Amplification Tests (NAATs) done at a nationally accredited/approved laboratory using  sample tested within 96 hours of departure to URT. All travelers, whether foreigners or returning residents arriving from countries  experiencing variants of concern/variants of interest, highest number of COVID-19 cases  based on WHO epidemiological updates or those who have travelled through those  countries in the last 14 days regardless of route taken shall be subjected to rapid tests at  Points of Entry at their own cost. Cost for COVID-19 rapid test shall be 10 USD or  TZS.23,000 of which is non-refundable and testing fees applies to traveler arriving in  Tanzania Mainland by Air or Marine Vessels. The program will benefit all the citizens of Tanzania by contributing to safeguarding the country’s macroeconomic stability, building resilience and supporting a more inclusive economic recovery. The direct beneficiaries from the program are various households, the private sector and participants in the government social protection program, notably TASAF. The program will assist with safeguarding the livelihoods of vulnerable women and men, through gender-inclusive social protection programs and COVID-19 relief schemes. The National Sanitation Campaign, named Nyumba Ni Choo, began its coronavirus response in March 2020. The campaign joined the World Health Organisation’s #SafeHands challenge in collaborating with celebrities from a wide range of sectors (including football players, musical artists, government officials and fashion models) to demonstrate good hand washing practice and encourage people to practice it regularly. Several of the celebrities also serve as campaign spokespeople.  The on-the-ground campaign was joined in person in April by Anyitike Mwakitalima, Head of the Water and Sanitation Unit from the MoHCDGEC. Anyitike and the campaign team conducted outreach in four regions of Tanzania, visiting local communities and raising awareness of the importance of handwashing with soap. The team met with the local population, religious leaders, business owners and local government officials while organising physically-distanced discussions and gathering testimonies of people’s experience of the escalating pandemic. The visits were covered by the media and footage was aired on national television and radio and shared in newspapers and on social media.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210926063929/https://hygienehub.info/en/case-studies/tanzania-celebrities-join-the-fight-against-covid-19
